introduction:
Traditional IT infrastructure setups and collaboration between development and operational teams are not in line with today’s agile methodologies and release strategies. Performing changes in a traditional infrastructure environment requires the involvement of multiple teams, sometimes from different companies. Even though engineers usually try to connect over the boundaries of teams and companies to find common ground, getting them all on the same page and at the same time is a real challenge. For that reason, project leaders try to find these common grounds and orchestrate calls with lots of people involved.
To overcome these problems, organizations form DevOps teams that improve collaboration between teams and introduce new processes in the organization. These DevOps teams are usually in charge of the whole application stack, including infrastructure components. DevOps teams achieve great results since they can work together without any traditional obstacles.
However, in recent years, problems around this approach have emerged while application environments that are build this way are running in production and trying to withstand the test of time. DevOps teams usually operate in line with development practices and traditional operational processes are lost this way. This causes problems in terms of life cycle management, security, availability, data protection, … We also see that developers in the DevOps teams start spending more and more time on operational related tasks, resulting in higher costs.
To overcome these problems, organizations setup development platforms that provide all the components development teams need without all the overhead of managing it themselves. The “platform team” providing the “developer platform” follows operational processes in terms of life cycle management, security audits, availability, data protection, …. This way of working is called “Platform Engineering”.
On first glance, this looks like a step back into the old ways of doing IT, however, the difference is in the self-service development teams have on that development platform. Development teams should not be blocked on requests for infra at any given time. The Platform team provides the platform and will provide the resources needed for development teams to provision the application environments they need. Development teams use Infrastructure As Code methodologies to provision the application environment inside the platform without involvement of the operations teams.
In this section, we will describe the benefits of using Cegeka’s Cloud-Native Platform for cloud native application development.
about the document:
This document is the service description of Cegeka’s Cloud-Native Platform.
The service description details what is part of the offered service and how the service is delivered. This includes the way the service is setup, how the service is managed after the setup and which types of service levels are provided when delivering the service.
It also describes the prerequisites which must be fulfilled to deliver the service and what subjects of the service are considered out-of-scope.
Finally, the document provides a comprehensive RACI, detailing the roles and responsibility for both Cegeka and the customer.
service introduction:
Cegeka’s Cloud-Native Platform enables customers to deploy scalable cloud native applications.
By using the provided container orchestrator and associated cloud native tooling, everything is at hand to build and deploy cloud native applications supporting DevSecOps principles which leads to shorter delivery times, scalable & flexible applications and increased automation.
Cegeka’s Cloud-Native Platform provides a managed service on 3 distinct layers:
Layer 1 - Managed infrastructure
A container platform relies on infrastructure. Compute, storage, networking components, etc… are all crucial components to host a container platform. With Cegeka’s Cloud-Native Platform, the infrastructure is fully managed as a service. The servicing includes but is not limited to provisioning, maintenance, life cycle management, incident management, problem management and overall support.
Cegeka’s Cloud-Native Platform can be deployed on two types of cloud infrastructure:
Layer 2 - Kubernetes container orchestration
The second layer is the container platform, hosted on top of the infrastructure layer. The container platform is based on Kubernetes, a community supported container orchestrator.
As part of Cegeka’s Cloud-Native Platform, the container orchestrator is fully managed as a service. Similar to Layer 1, this servicing includes provisioning, maintenance, lifecycle management, incident management, problem management and overall support.
Next to the container orchestration layer, the platform is enriched with standard tooling to support running applications on the platform. Layer 2 provides:
Layer 3 - Cloud-native tooling
Cloud native applications are built, deployed, managed, and observed using an ecosystem of tools. Besides, these cloud native applications typically consisting of different microservices, rely on technologies that make it possible to ensure systems are loosely coupled (eg. message busses, API gateways,…)
Cegeka’s Cloud-Native Platform Layer 3 aims to deliver this ecosystem in an easy and integrated way, ensuring it is fully managed and kept up to date. As part of the service, only the technical management of the tools is delivered, and no functional management is part of the service.
The following tooling categories are available - for a customer to chose from - and can be added to the  platform. Each supported tool is discussed in detail in chapter 4.3
service deployment types:
The service is available in 4 deployment types: Local Redundant, Zone Redundant (Azure public cloud only), Geo-Zone Redundant (Azure public cloud only) and Disaster Recovery (Cegeka private cloud only). Below you can find a high-level description of these deployment types, detailing the general use cases for each type:
On network level, the firewall and loadbalancer are streched over both datacenters and provide seamless failover in case of disaster. Clusters are located in dedicated VLANs which are only used within that site. Storage replication is performed on the application level. Selected data inside the platform is actively synced over the two datacenters in an asynchronous way to enable failover to the other datacenter.
Illustration: Local Redundant vs Disaster Recovery
On network level, the layer 2 VLANs are contained within a single site, failover to the other site is handled at the IP layer (Layer 3). This network setup is the most robust one and recommended. Selected data inside the platform is actively synced in an asynchronous way over the 2 geo-locations to enable failover to the other geolocation.
Illustration: Zone Redundant vs Geo-Zone Redundant

platform setup: Cegeka’s Cloud-Native Platform is automatically provisioned by Cegeka and consists of a number of components, which will be discussed in detail in the following sections.
platform foundation:
The Platform Foundation is an essential and mandatory component of the service, dedicated per customer and deployed once per geo location. It contains the network layer, security components (eg. a firewall and web application firewall, loadbalancer) and the management cluster used by Cegeka to provision and manage the customer’s environment. Note: the management cluster is not accessible by the customer.
Once the Platform Foundation is in place, all required components are available to start deploying workload clusters.
workload clusters:
One or more workload clusters can be added to the customer’s environment. As an example, the diagram below illustrates the deployment and availability of a single workload cluster. A workload cluster consist of different types of nodes: System nodes are not suited, nor available for hosting customer applications and are crucial to the cluster at any given moment. Just like the system nodes, tooling nodes are not suited, nor available for hosting customer applications. Node pools For each of these types of nodes, there is a corresponding node pool. Node pools are required to avoid interference between the customer’s applications and the services provided and managed by Cegeka. A node pool is a logical group of nodes marked for a specific purpose and is sized based on the load it must sustain. The number of nodes within each node pool are scalable in function of application resource demand. Node sizing Sizing of the tooling nodes depends on the amount of tools deployed within the platform. By default a minimum of 2x tooling nodes are included in a workload cluster. If more resources are needed, extra tooling nodes will be added to the platform. The number of tooling nodes will also take into account high availability to withstand the loss of at least 1 tooling node. For bigger tools like Elasticsearch or a heavy loaded Apache Kafka cluster, additional dedicated node pools can be created. The sizing of these nodes will then be calculated based on the needs of the tool. Sizing of the user nodes depends on the requirements of the running application(s): some applications are more CPU intensive, others more memory demanding. The memory to cpu -ratio can be adjusted for user node pools. As a baseline configuration, we typically use nodes with 8 CPU’s and 32GB of memory, with an optimal resource load of 70-80% during usage. By default a minimum of 2x user nodes are included in a workload cluster. If the Cloud-Native Platform is deployed on Cegeka private cloud infrastructure, persistent storage volumes are created through the use of the Ceph cloud native tool or by integrating with Global Ops storage services. Ceph1 provides a unified solution for object storage, block storage, and file storage within a single cluster. Using Ceph requires a number of dedicated storage nodes for every workload cluster. If integration with the shared GlobalOps storage solutions is selected, this is added as another product next to the Cloud-Native Platform. 1 Storage volumes will be discussed in detail in section 4.1.3 | type | Min amount of nodes | compute (cpu) per node | memory (GB) per node | storage (GB) per node | | tooling nodes | 2 | 8 | 32 | 100 | | user nodes | 2 | 2 - 16 | 16 - 64 | 100-200 | | storage nodes 1 | 4 | 2 - 16 3 | 12 – 64 3 | 500 4 - Ceph storage volumes 2 |

Logging Handling logging on a container platform is not as simple as handling logging on a traditional server. Logs cannot be stored in the container (since it might lose data when restarted) or on a node, since the container might be running on another node a few minutes later. To solve this, logs are collected from containers and stored in a log aggregation tool. Different solutions are available, but we have chosen to use Grafana Loki by default. When using Grafana Loki, the Loki datasource will be added to the Grafana dashboards in the platform. This means that dashboards with metrics and logging information in the same dashboard can be made. This section describes these different features. Container Registry To be able to build and deploy custom created container images, a container registry is needed. Most customers will have a centralized container registry like Sonatype Nexus, but to make sure a developer is able to locally create and test images, a light-weight container registry is part of the platform. | Name | Description | | Docker Container Registry | A Docker Container Registry plays a crucial role in a Kubernetes cluster by serving as a centralized repository for storing and managing Docker images. It allows developers to easily share and distribute containerized applications and services. By hosting images in a Docker Container Registry, the Kubernetes cluster can efficiently pull the required images for pod creation, streamline deployment workflows, and enhance security through image scanning and access controls. |

Service Mesh A service mesh is a tool for adding observability, security, and reliability features to applications by inserting these features at the platform layer rather than the application layer. The service mesh is typically implemented as a scalable set of network proxies deployed alongside application code (a pattern sometimes called a sidecar). These proxies handle the communication between the microservices and act as a point at which the service mesh features can be introduced. The proxies comprise the service mesh’s data plane and are controlled by its control plane. | Name | Description | | Istio | Istio is an open-source service mesh platform that provides traffic management, security, and observability features for microservices running in a Kubernetes environment. Ingress Controller To support incoming traffic into the platform, an ingress controller must be deployed inside the container platform. This ingress controller will handle incoming traffic and route it to the correct application. This ingress controller consists out of 2 components. The first one is the loadbalancer, which is traditionally provided by an external component to the platform (Haproxy, Loadbalancer appliance, …). We selected the BigIP F5 loadbalacer for the task to provide a robuust and stable entry point to the platform. The 2nd part is the ingress controller. This runs on the platform and will receive requests from the loadbalancer. The ingress controller is fully aware of the current state of the kubernetes cluster and will be able to route any traffic to the correct application pods. For the platform, we decided to use the ingress controller from the service mesh Istio, which is already part of the platform. Certificate Manager - Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) To secure endpoints in a container platform, you need certificates. Generating and managing these certificates can be time consuming and risky (certificate expiration). As part of the platform, we provide a component that creates and manages the certificates that are used by your endpoints. In the base setup of the platform, a base configuration with a self-signed root-certificate is used. All certificates will be signed with this platform root certificate. This system secures your platform for development and test use. However, if required, integration with the customer’s PKI or an Internet CA (eg. Deployment and GitOps GitOps is an operational framework that takes DevOps best practices used for application development such as version control, collaboration, compliance, and CI/CD tooling, and applies them to infrastructure automation. GitOps main ability is to control what is deployed and to control it afterwards. Any change to the infrastructure managed by GitOps must be done to the source control which describes the environment (Infrastructure As Code). security & connectivity components:
Vault The way secrets are stored in Kubernetes is not really secure. Secrets are base64 encoded when stored, but you can easily reverse the encryption. To store secrets in a secure way in Kubernetes, you need a secure Vault inside the platform. | Name | Description | | HashiCorp Vault | HashiCorp Vault is an identity-based secrets and encryption management system. A secret is anything that you want to tightly control access to, such as API encryption keys, passwords, and certificates. Vault provides encryption services that are gated by authentication and authorization methods. |
Name Description HashiCorp Vault HashiCorp Vault is an identity-based secrets and encryption management system. A secret is anything that you want to tightly control access to, such as API encryption keys, passwords, and certificates. Vault provides encryption services that are gated by authentication and authorization methods. Hashicorp Vault requires a license when it is added to the Cloud-Native Platform. Security Scanning Container orchestration systems like Kubernetes provide a simpler way to set up and manage containers at scale. As container adoption has increased, usage of these orchestration systems has skyrocketed. Kubernetes, for example, provides orchestration for more than three-quarters of containerized applications today. Of course, the popularity of these platforms makes them common targets for cyber criminals. Kubernetes combats this risk by offering pod security policies and drift management to help secure containers in development — but what happens when containers go to runtime? While Docker and Kubernetes offer numerous tools for building security into containers, they don’t secure the runtime environment itself. As a result, a rich runtime stack of tools, processes, and policies has emerged that does the work of securing containers in production. To provide the highest level op protection, Cegeka incorporates the Cegeka C-SOR²C services as part of the platform. With our Cyber Security Operations, Response & Recovery Center (C-SOR²C), Cegeka offers a modern SOC, drawing upon our extensive security expertise and with R² we add Response and Recover to Security Operations via Comprehensive cyber security services. We deliver a wide range of cyber security services that assist in detecting, responding to and recovering from attacks: Web Application Firewall Cloud-native WAF is like an entry point for all web and internet-based applications which is configured as a reverse proxy. It uses the scalability and computation of cloud infrastructure to detect the security challenges and quickly isolate them from the threat. | Name | Description | | BigIP F5 | A web application firewall (WAF) protects web applications from a variety of application layer attacks such as cross-site scripting (XSS), SQL injection, and cookie poisoning, among others. The platform will integrate with the BigIP F5 which makes it possible to define and configured WAF policies inside the Kubernetes platform. |
Name Description BigIP F5 A web application firewall (WAF) protects web applications from a variety of application layer attacks such as cross-site scripting (XSS), SQL injection, and cookie poisoning, among others. API Gateway API Gateway is an essential component in the full lifecycle API management. It is responsible for API configuration, release, version rollback, security, and load balancing in the production environment. In addition, API gateway is the entrance of all client traffic, responsible for routing the client’s API request to the correct upstream service for processing and then returning the returned data to the original requester while ensuring safety, reliability, and low latency of the entire process. | Name | Description | | APISIX | APISIX is a dynamic, high-performance API gateway designed for microservices, cloud-native, and hybrid cloud architectures. It offers features such as load balancing, dynamic upstream, canary release, circuit breaking, and authentication. | | Kong | Kong is a popular, open-source API gateway and microservices management layer that facilitates the creation, deployment, and management of APIs. It offers a robust set of features including load balancing, caching, logging, request/response transformation, authentication, and rate limiting. For Enterprise plugins, Kong requires a license. |

Caching In traditional applications, temporary data like session data is stored inside the application (usually in memory or in a local db/file). In modern applications, were scaling needs to be possible, storing data inside a running instance is not an option since you are not sure on what instance the next call will arrive. To avoid loss of data between instances, data is stored outside the instance in centralised systems. If temporary data must be stored, storing the data in a caching system is a good option. | Name | Description | | Redis | Redis is an open-source, in-memory data structure store known for its speed and versatility, commonly used as a cache, message broker, or database. Each provisioned instance of the product is deployed with a certain version. New versions are released frequently, and updates are pushed regularly towards the running Cloud-Native Platform instances.

security management:
Security is a very important part of the platform. Some security features are there to keep the platform itself secure, others are available to the customer to keep applications running on the platform secure.
To provide the highest level op protection, Cegeka will incorporate the Cegeka C-SOR²C services as part of the platform. With our Cyber Security Operations, Response & Recovery Center (C-SOR²C), Cegeka offers a modern SOC, drawing upon our extensive security expertise and with R² we add Response and Recover to Security Operations via Comprehensive cyber security services.
We deliver a wide range of cyber security services that assist in detecting, responding to and recovering from attacks:
Based on the requirements of the customer some or all services will be included in the platform.
In this section we describe the security features that are integrated with the platform:
build-in firewall: The platform is deployed with its own firewall cluster. This firewall cluster is securely designed and uses all security best-practices.
vulnerability scanning:
Vulnerability scanning is the process of discovering, analysing, and reporting on security flaws and vulnerabilities. Vulnerability scans are conducted via automated vulnerability scanning tools to identify potential risk exposures and attack vectors across an organization’s networks, hardware, software, and systems. Vulnerability scanning and assessment is an essential step in the vulnerability management lifecycle.
Cegeka has a vulnerability scanning service that is part of the Cegeka security stack. The platform and all of its components will be scanned at regular intervals and actions are taken if urgent vulnerabilities pop-up as part of these scans.
A vulnerability dashboard will be provided towards the customer with results of applications scans running on the platform (runtime scanning). This dashboard is informative, and no actions will be taken by Cegeka based on the results.
cis compliancy:
CIS (Center for Internet Security) is a forward-thinking non-profit that harnesses the power of a global IT community to safeguard public and private organizations against cyber threats.
CIS provides security benchmarks for all sorts of infrastructure components (eg. OS, Kubernetes).  The CIS Controls CIS sets as part of their benchmarks are seen as the reference for securing infrastructure systems.
the Cloud-Native Platform is designed with CIS compliancy in mind. This means that the Security Controls that CIS provides are built into the platform. To make sure these Controls are not changed between releases or at runtime, CIS benchmark scans are performed at a regular basis.
service mesh: Microservices have particular security needs, including protection against man-in-the-middle attacks, flexible access controls, auditing tools, and mutual TLS.  These needs can be met by using a service mesh. This service mesh can provide strong identity, powerful policy, transparent TLS encryption, and authentication, authorization and audit tools to protect your services and data.
regulatory compliancy:
Navigating the continually evolving regulatory landscape of data privacy and security presents significant challenges for organizations. Cegeka’s Compliant Cloud can give assistance in adhering to these regulations, providing businesses peace of mind when it comes to their data and business resilience.
The Cloud-Native Platform offering is in line with Cegeka’s Compliant Cloud principles.
reporting: Thorough reporting ensures the customer is informed about the platform and its performance as a whole. Information on the platform components, the platform’s availability, known & resolved incidents, executed changes and SLA performance all form a crucial part in achieving fully transparent communication.
component information: The platform comes with a documentation portal. The documentation portal dynamically shows all components part of the set-up, giving a clear overview of what is included in the set-up and making these components easily accessible. When additional (optional) cloud-native tools are added, these will appear in the documentation portal as well.
monitoring: Via built-in Grafana dashboards, the customer can monitor the cluster nodes and cloud-native tools. Being the industry standard and because of the included Prometheus instance, a customer can easily create new dashboards and alerts to monitor its applications. Prometheus can be integrated with a wide range of external alert receivers.
incidents:
Being a proactive service, the Cloud-Native Platform will automatically detect infra-, container platform or tooling incidents via the monitoring instances in place. Once detected, this will invoke the creation of a ticket. For P1 and P2 incidents, the customer will be informed via e-mail about these incidents.
In case of an additional incident, issue, problem or feature request, a ticket can always be created via Horizon by the customer. Via Cegeka’s ticketing system, all issues can be followed up closely. Additionally, a history of resolved and closed tickets is available to review past platform performance.
changes:
Standard change requests can be requested via Horizon. Afterwards, the status of the change requests can be consulted in Horizon. This will help the customer keep track of which changes have been executed over time. infrastructure_management: The Cloud-Native Platform must be deployed on a Cegeka managed Layer 1 “Core infrastructure”. The following underlying platforms are supported:
capacity management:
A container platform is able to optimize hardware investments to the fullest since it can shift workload around and use all available resources to the maximum. Workloads might grow over time and cause high pressure on the platform. This can be CPU or memory pressure. If CPU or memory pressure is too high, the platform might become unstable or cause more than normal application restarts to keep the cluster out of problems.
As part of the service, the capacity of the platform will be closely monitored. If the platform load is too high, the customer will be requested to limit the load or to extend the platform by adding more resources. If this request is denied and the load stays high on the platform, SLA will shift to best-effort and business hours only.
lifecycle management:
The platform support is based on running the latest version of the platform. Regular updates will be pushed to the platform. This will be done outside business hours and communicated in advance to the customer. No impact analysis for the updates will be done for the applications running on the platform. A changelog will be provided to the customer in advance and updates will be delivered according to the DTAP configuration of the customer to allow the possibility of testing for the customer.
If, however, the customer refuses updates, and the version of the platform falls outside the Cegeka supported versions, the SLA will shift to best-effort and business hours only. Upgrading to a supported version will result in extra costs.

self-service:
A key aspect of the Cloud-Native Platform is the self-service capabilities for its users. Users should be able to perform any necessary task without being blocked by manual processes like traditional change processes. However, providing users full access and control to infrastructure is dangerous and might lead to outages. The best way to achieve user agility with limited risk is to use an IaC link between the platform and the infrastructure. Traditional infrastructure components like storage, loadbalancers, certificates, … are provisioned from code inserted into the platform by the application deployment system. Development teams will thus be able to provision all the resources they need without requesting infrastructure components. If resources are not needed anymore, they are deleted from the code and that will result in a decom action of the infrastructure resources.
In this section, we will go over these different infrastructure components that can be provisioned using IaC inside the platform.
storage:
As discussed, the platform contains 3 types of storage: block, blob and file storage. In a traditional setup, storage is provisioned to VM’s on request of application teams and provided by infrastructure teams. In this setup, the infrastructure team provides a certain amount of storage to the platform. The development team is able to provision the application storage with infrastructure as code inside the platform.
When the storage isn’t needed anymore, the definition is deleted from the platform, leading to a decommission action on the storage system.
To achieve this, Kubernetes uses CSI’s (Container Storage Interfaces) to connect to different storage providers. The Cloud-Native Platform provides a dynamic storage layer (with Ceph) that provides dynamic and flexible storage to the platform. All 3 types of storage can be provisioned using a CSI in the platform.
network components:
The platform comes with a set of firewalls pre-configured. These firewall rules are configured based on what the platform needs to operate. They also include rules that er needed for applications to be available and reachable. In the future, it will also expose ports based on configuration in the container platform (e.g. when a VIP is created).
Using this way of working can significantly reduce costs of network actions.
certificates:
Creating, signing, replacing, monitoring, … certificates can be very time consuming. It can even cause outages when an imminent expiring certificate goes undetected.
The platform provides a component that manages these certificates (if it is supported by the CA). An internal PKI can be integrated with the platform or an internet CA like Let’s Encrypt, so the platform can sign certificates and assign them to applications. The Certificate manager will also replace certificates in time (after 2/3th of its lifetime) automatically and delete them if they are no longer needed. Not every internet CA supports this system yet, but the CA’s that do support the system are growing (DigiCert, Let’s Encrypt, Google Trust Services, Entrust, Sectigo, …)
Using this system takes away all the work around managing certificates and can result in a massive increase of cost savings related to certificate management.
backup: The backup of data inside the platform will integrate with the Cegeka backup system based on Commvault. Backup policies can be selected for data inside the platform, de-worrying developers on data protection.
tooling configuration:
Cloud Native tooling in a Cloud Native Application Platform includes observability tooling, message busses, API gateways, Vaults, Caching, Orchestration, Security components, …
Getting all of these components configured in a traditional platform would require different teams and dozens of changes.
the Cloud-Native Platform provides ways to automatically configure every tool in the platform through IaC or API calls. Popular provisioning tools like terraform and ansible can be used for several tools inside the platform. For most tools, a GUI is available that provides easy insights into the configuration and can increase the learning curve significantly. This way of working enables full automation of an application environment via IaC.
Deploying applications this way, can massively decrease deployment costs and increase the time to market significantly.
operator based automation:
Operators in Kubernetes can be compared with a perfect engineer, looking at the platform 247. It will detect certain situations and act on them with predefined actions. These perfect engineers can be used to make the life of users a lot easier. Well known operators are CSI’s (Container Storage Interfaces) and Certificate manager.
Cloud native tools sometimes lack features that make them easy to use in all circumstances and the CI/CD pipeline should contain logic to overcome these issues, however, this isn’t always simple. Our product development team designs and created new operators in the platform to overcome these problems and to unburden the customer (and thus the CI/CD pipeline).
prerequisites: The deployment of the platform is depended on some prerequisites:
learning curve:
The platform usage guide is available on a documentation portal. This documentation portal provides insight into the platform and its components. Every component is described, and examples are provided to use the components in an automated way. The platform team will be available to answer questions and will continuously improve the documentation, based on the feedback of the users.
This makes sure that teams are able to make a kickstart using the platform and provide a lower learning curve. Understanding how to use the platform should be easy based on the documentation and the interfaces provided by the platform. This way, the user can focus on the functional aspect of the application instead of the technical aspect of the components.
platform value: The Cloud-Native Platform enables teams to define infrastructure components and tooling in an IaC way enabling DevOps teams to deliver applications in an agile way resulting in a much lower costs and faster time to market. This will result in a higher return on investment.
product development:
The platform delivered will be improved constantly with bugfixes, patches and new features. The product development team will keep following the Cloud Native Community and, based on user feedback, will keep adding new features and functionality to the platform. New versions of the platform will thus not only contain new versions of the tooling, but might also include a new feature that is delivered out of the box.
